content.problemStatement:
Chef is given an array A=[A1,A2,…,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N]A=[A1​,A2​,…,AN​] of size NNN. He considers an array AAA to be GoodGoodGood if no two adjacent elements of AAA are equal (((i.e Ai≠Ai+1A_i \neq A_{i+1}Ai​=Ai+1​ for all 1≤i<N)1\leq i<N)1≤i<N), and it has at most one local maximum and at most one local minimum.

For an array [A1,A2,…,AN][A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N][A1​,A2​,…,AN​] an index iii (1<i<N)(1 < i < N)(1<i<N) is said to be a local minimum if Ai<min{Ai−1,Ai+1}A_i<min\{A_{i-1}, A_{i+1}\}Ai​<min{Ai−1​,Ai+1​}. Similarly, an index iii (1<i<N)(1<i<N)(1<i<N) is said to be a local maximum if Ai>max{Ai−1,Ai+1}A_i>max\{A_{i-1}, A_{i+1}\}Ai​>max{Ai−1​,Ai+1​}. 

Note that the indices 111 and NNN can neither be a local maximum nor a local minimum.

Chef can perform the following two operations on the array any number of times:


Choose two indices iii and jjj (1≤i,j≤N)(1\leq i, j \leq N)(1≤i,j≤N) and change AiA_iAi​ to AjA_jAj​. Performing this operation costs 000.
Choose any index iii (1≤i≤N)(1\leq i\leq N)(1≤i≤N) and replace AiA_iAi​ with any positive integer. Performing this operation costs KKK.
After performing the above operations some number of times (possibly zero), chef can rearrange the array however he wants. This rearrangement at the end doesn't cost anything.

Help chef determine the minimum cost required to make the array GoodGoodGood.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers, NNN and KKK — the size of the array and the cost of the second operation.
The next line contains NNN integers representing the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the minimum cost required to make the array AAA GoodGoodGood.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 5⋅1055 \cdot 10^55⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Testcase 1: The given array is A=[1,1,1]A=[1, 1, 1]A=[1,1,1]. Since A1=A2A_1 = A_2A1​=A2​ and A2=A3A_2 = A_3A2​=A3​, it is currently not GoodGoodGood. 
We can apply the second operation, choose the index 222 and change A2A_2A2​ to 2. The resulting array is [1,2,1][1, 2, 1][1,2,1] which is GoodGoodGood.
We can show that this is the minimum cost needed to convert the original array into a GoodGoodGood array, and hence the answer is 222.
Testcase 2: The given array is A=[3,1,2,4]A=[3, 1, 2, 4]A=[3,1,2,4]. No two adjacent elements are equal.
A2=1A_2 = 1A2​=1 is the only local minima in the array, and there are no local maximas at all.
Hence the given array is already GoodGoodGood. Therefore, the cost needed to change it is 000.
Testcase 3: The given array is A=[1,1,2,2,3]A=[1, 1, 2, 2, 3]A=[1,1,2,2,3]. Since A1=A2A_1 = A_2A1​=A2​ and A3=A4A_3 = A_4A3​=A4​, it is currently not GoodGoodGood. 
But without performing any operations, we directly go to rearranging the array, and rearrange it into [1,2,1,2,3][1, 2, 1, 2, 3][1,2,1,2,3].
Now, no two adjacent elements are equal.
A3=1A_3 = 1A3​=1 is the only local minima in the array, and A2=2A_2 = 2A2​=2 is the only local maxima in the array.
Hence the rearranged array is GoodGoodGood. But since the rearrangement is free of cost, the minimim cost needed to change the original array into a GoodGoodGood array is 000.
Note that we could also have rearranged it to [1,3,2,1,2][1, 3, 2, 1, 2][1,3,2,1,2] or [2,1,3,2,1][2, 1, 3, 2, 1][2,1,3,2,1]. Both of these examples also have a cost of 000.
